When writing tests, utilize the NUnit testing framework, along with its assertions.

Focus on tests that assert that the output images generated by the library match
expected output images based on pre-defined input images and scripts.  The tests should
cover a wide range of scenarios, including various transformations and combinations of
input images, as well as error handling for invalid scripts or input images.  The tests
should be designed to be comprehensive and to provide confidence in the robustness and
reliability of the library.

Do not write tests that merely assert specific implementation details of the library,
such as the internal structure of the code or the specific algorithms used for transformations.
Instead, focus on testing the overall functionality and behavior of the library from the
perspective of a user who is writing scripts to generate output images.  The tests should be
designed to verify that the library correctly parses the scripts, applies the specified
transformations, and generates output images that match the expected results based on the
input images and scripts provided.

The tests will be written in a separate test project named Scrapbook.Tests, which will reference
the main library project.  The test project will be organized into multiple test classes,
each focusing on a specific aspect of the library's functionality, such as parsing, transformations,
and error handling.  Each test class will contain multiple test methods that cover various scenarios
and edge cases related to that aspect of the library.

When writing tests that include specific colours that are part of input images and then asserted
to exist in the output images, assign meaningful names to those colours in the test code to improve
readability and maintainability.  For example, if a specific shade of red is used in the input image
and is expected to be present in the output image, you could define a variable name to that colour
so that is clear to anyone reading the test code what that colour represents and why it is important
for the test.  This will will be much easier to read than seeing a specific RGB value in the test code
without any context or explanation.

Test cases should include invalid copy bounds, invalid input index, malformed script syntax, etc. in
addition to valid scenarios.  This will ensure that the library's error handling is robust and provides
appropriate feedback for users when they encounter issues with their scripts or input images.
By covering a wide range of scenarios, including both valid and invalid cases, the tests will help to
ensure that the library is reliable and can handle a variety of situations that users may encounter when
using it to generate output images based on their scripts and input images.

If you see a folder named Requirements in the Scrapbook.Tests project, do not write or touch tests in
that folder without explicit instructions.
That folder is intended for human review of the requirements and is not meant for automated testing.
Instead, write all tests in other locations in the Scrapbook.Tests project, which is intended for 
automated testing of the library's functionality and behavior.
